meta.title: E28 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 5 Series E28 engine — M10, M20, M30, M88/S38 M5, M21 diesel. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 5 Series E28 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E28 engine typically costs £1,200–£9,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on the engine — the four-cylinder M10 sits at £1,200–£2,500, the M20 and M30 six-cylinders at £1,800–£5,500, and the M5's M88/S38 at £5,000–£9,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,170. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which E28 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M30B28 (528i) is the E28's most dependable engine — 181 bhp of silky, naturally-aspirated straight-six with a proven track record of 200,000+ miles. The M20B27 'eta' (525e/528e) is also famously durable, with many examples exceeding 200,000 miles. The M21 diesel is robust but increasingly rare.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 5 Series E28?

acceptedAnswer.text: The cooling system is the E28's single biggest weakness — water pumps fail, radiators corrode, and expansion tanks crack on all six-cylinder models. The M20's timing belt (520i, 525e) is an interference engine — failure destroys the engine. The M5's M88/S38 timing chain guides wear and require expensive engine-out replacement. The M21 diesel's injection pump is its primary weak point.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 5 Series E28 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the E28 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most robust cars. The M20 and M30 six-cylinders are famously durable, with many examples exceeding 200,000 miles. The cooling system is the primary weakness, but it's well-understood and manageable. The M5 is more demanding but fundamentally strong. Regular maintenance is the key — these are 40+ year old cars now.

mainEntity.name: Which E28 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but an M20 without documented timing belt history is a risk (interference engine). An M5 without documented timing chain guide history is a serious financial risk. A 524td without injection pump history is also a gamble. Avoid any E28 with obvious overheating damage or poor service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E28 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Overheating (cooling system failure), timing chain rattle (M5), timing belt failure (M20 — catastrophic), hard starting or loss of power (M21 diesel injection pump), or persistent coolant loss are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E28 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £6,000 528i, a £2,500–£4,500 recon is 42–75% of the car's value — at the upper end, that brushes our 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a £4,000 520i, a £1,800–£3,500 recon is 45–88% — borderline. For an M5 at £15,000+, a £5,000–£9,000 recon is 33–60% — a serious decision, but these are appreciating classics. Value the car and its condition first; if it's an M20 without timing belt history, walk away or budget for the work immediately.

mainEntity.name: How long do E28 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M20 and M30 six-cylinders can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — the cooling system is the limiting factor. The M21 diesel is similarly durable. The M88/S38 M5 engines are capable of high mileage but require more frequent maintenance — timing chain guides, valve adjustments, and cooling systems all need attention at 60k–100k mile intervals.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E12, E28, and E34 generations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E28 (1981–1988) is the second-generation 5 Series, succeeding the E12 (1972–1981) and preceding the E34 (1988–1996). The E28 introduced the angled centre console towards the driver, ABS, and was the first 5 Series with a diesel engine. The E12 is more classic and analogue; the E34 is more refined and modern. The E28 sits in the middle — more capable than the E12, more characterful than the E34. The E28 M5 was the world's fastest production saloon at launch.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E28 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For petrol four-cylinders: M10B18 (518/518i). For petrol six-cylinders: M20B20 (520i), M20B27 (525e/528e), M30B28 (528i), M30B30 (530i US), M30B32 (533i US), M30B34 (535i/M535i), M88/3 (M5 Euro), S38B35 (M5 US/SA). For diesels: M21D24 (524d), M21D24 Turbo (524td). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 5 Series E28 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E28 saloon — from the four-cylinder M10 to the six-cylinder M20, M30, and the M88/S38-powered M5 that started a legend. Real UK replacement costs and honest reliability data for BMW's second-generation 5 Series. Backed by real UK enquiries [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 5 Series E28 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E28 specifically — including the carburetted M10, the full range of M20 and M30 six-cylinders, and the M88/S38 that powered the original M5.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any E28 engine family. The M20 and M30 six-cylinders are well-regarded for their robustness, with the M30B28 (528i) considered the sweet spot for everyday usability. The M20B27 "eta" engine is famously durable but low-revving. The M88/3 and S38B35 are high-strung performance engines — fundamentally strong but require diligent maintenance. The M21 diesel is robust but increasingly rare.

overview.intro: The E28 is the second generation of the BMW 5 Series, produced from 1981 to 1988. Designed by Claus Luthe, it succeeded the E12 and introduced a more aerodynamic, modern silhouette. The E28 was initially offered with straight-four and straight-six petrol engines. In 1983, the 524td became the first BMW car to be powered by a diesel engine. The E28 was also the first 5 Series with the centre console angled towards the driver and the first to offer anti-lock brakes (ABS). The first BMW M5 was built on the E28 platform, powered by the M88/3 engine derived from the M1 supercar. The E28 was replaced by the E34 in 1988. In total, 722,328 E28s were produced.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E28 (front-engine, rear-wheel drive) • Predecessor: BMW 5 Series (E12) • Successor: BMW 5 Series (E34) • Sibling body styles in this generation: 4-door saloon only
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The 5 Series nameplate generated significant UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with the six-cylinder E28 models (520i, 525i, 528i, 535i, M5) representing the majority of E28-related demand based on market patterns.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre M20 six-cylinder delivers 125 bhp — enough for everyday driving with six-cylinder smoothness and better fuel economy than the larger M30s. Parts are cheap and widely available. It's the sensible choice that still feels special.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder refinement on a budget.

items.quote: The 286 bhp M88/3 is a masterpiece — derived from the M1 supercar — but it's high-strung and expensive to maintain. Timing chain guides, valve adjustments, and cooling system work are all costly. Parts are becoming increasingly scarce.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who understand the cost of ownership.

items.quote: A full M88/3 or S38B35 engine rebuild can exceed £8,000. Timing chain guide failure is catastrophic and requires an engine-out rebuild. Parts are rare and specialist labour is essential. The M5 is a legend — but it's an expensive one to keep on the road.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want one of the greatest saloon cars ever made.

items.quote: The 'eta' engine is often dismissed as underpowered — 121–129 bhp from 2.7 litres — but it's designed for torque and economy, not revs. It's famously durable, with many examples exceeding 200,000 miles. It's the sensible choice that gets overlooked by those chasing power.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise longevity and economy over outright performance.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Budget Choice — cheap to run, low power
Definitive E28 — the one to buy
Rare US Model — short production run
Top Non-M — 218 bhp, smooth and torquey
US M5 — slightly detuned, still costly
First BMW Diesel — robust and economical
Rare NA Diesel — underpowered but durable

economicsRule.title: The E28 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,500 M30 recon against a £3,000–£10,000 E28 sits at 25–150% of the car's value — on a sub‑£4,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a well‑kept £6,000+ example, the same recon drops to 42–75% — borderline but often viable for a cherished car. For an M5 at £15,000+, a £5,000–£9,000 recon is 33–60% — a serious decision, but these are appreciating classics. Value the car first; if it's a 518i without service history, walk away.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E28 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M20B20 (520i) — six-cylinder refinement, cheap parts, good economy.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E28 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M88/3 (M5) — timing chain guides, valve adjustments, parts scarcity — all expensive.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E28 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M30B28 (528i) — 181 bhp, silky smooth, proven reliability, and often cheaper than a 535i.

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (All Six-Cylinders)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying any six‑cylinder E28, budget for a full cooling system refresh immediately — £400–£900 is cheap insurance against a £2,500+ replacement. Replace the water pump, thermostat, and radiator. On an M5, this is non‑negotiable.

problems.title: M20 Timing Belt Failure (520i, 525e)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an M20-powered E28 without documented timing belt history, budget for immediate replacement — £300–£600 is dramatically cheaper than the £2,000+ replacement that follows a failure. On a 520i worth under £3,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

problems.title: M88/S38 Timing Chain Guides (M5)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an E28 M5, budget for timing chain guide replacement if there's no documented history — £2,000–£4,000 is cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows a failure. On an appreciating classic, this is a cost of ownership you should budget for.

problems.title: M21 Diesel Injection Pump Wear (524td, 524d)
problems.recommendation: If your 524td shows signs of hard starting or reduced power, have the injection pump inspected promptly — a £500–£1,000 refurbishment is manageable; a full replacement often isn't justified given the vehicle's age and value.

replacementCosts.h2: E28 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E28 only. The 525e and 528e are the same "eta" engine with different market badging — the 525e was sold in Europe, the 528e in North America. The 530i and 533i were US-market only models. The M5 was available globally with the M88/3 (Europe) or S38B35 (North America, South Africa).
